semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: \label{morphism decomposition} Let $(\mathcal{C},\tp,-.-,P)$ be a category with attributes.; Let $f$ be a semantic context morphism $\Gamma \to \Delta.B$, where $B$ is a semantic type in $\Delta$.; Moreover, if $f$ is of the form: $$\Gamma \xrightarrow{b}\Gamma.B[g]\xrightarrow{g.B}\Delta.B$$ for some semantic context morphism $\Gamma \xrightarrow{g}\Delta$ and some semantic term $b$ of $B[g]$, then the semantic term $\overline{f}$ is $b$ itself (observe that: $$P_Bf=P_B(g.B)b=gP_{B[g]}b=g$$ hence in this case $\Gamma.B[P_Bf]=\Gamma.B[g]$, so that the equality $\overline{f}=b$ typechecks). Conclusion: Then there is a unique semantic term $\overline{f}$ of $B[P_Bf]$ in $\Gamma$ such that the diagram: \[\begin{tikzcd} \Gamma \arrow[drrr, bend left, "f"] \arrow[dr, "\overline{f}"'] \\ & \Gamma.B[P_Bf] \arrow[rr, "P_Bf.B"'] && \Delta.B \end{tikzcd}\] commutes. \proof By cartesianity of $P$. \endproof

context_pack.section: Recap on categories with attributes
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop}\label{morphism decomposition} Let $(\mathcal{C},\tp,-.-,P)$ be a category with attributes. Let $f$ be a semantic context morphism $\Gamma \to \Delta.B$, where $B$ is a semantic type in $\Delta$. Then there is a unique semantic term $\overline{f}$ of $B[P_Bf]$ in $\Gamma$ such that the diagram: \[\begin{tikzcd} \Gamma \arrow[drrr, bend left, "f"] \arrow[dr, "\overline{f}"'] \\ & \Gamma.B[P_Bf] \arrow[rr, "P_Bf.B"'] && \Delta.B \end{tikzcd}\] commutes. Moreover, if $f$ is of the form: $$\Gamma \xrightarrow{b}\Gamma.B[g]\xrightarrow{g.B}\Delta.B$$ for some semantic context morphism $\Gamma \xrightarrow{g}\Delta$ and some semantic term $b$ of $B[g]$, then the semantic term $\overline{f}$ is $b$ itself (observe that: $$P_Bf=P_B(g.B)b=gP_{B[g]}b=g$$ hence in this case $\Gamma.B[P_Bf]=\Gamma.B[g]$, so that the equality $\overline{f}=b$ typechecks). \proof By cartesianity of $P$. \endproof \end{prop}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
For a semantic term $a$ of type $A$ in context $\Gamma$ and a semantic morphism $\Delta\xrightarrow{f}\Gamma$, the semantic terms $a[f]$ and $\overline{af}$ coincide.

\proof
It holds that $af=(f.A)a[f]$. Hence by \autoref{morphism decomposition} $\overline{af}$ is $a[f]$.
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{functoriality of a}
For a semantic term $a$, the semantic term $a[f]$ is functorial in $f$.


\proof The term $a[1_\Gamma]$ of $A[1_\Gamma]=A$ makes the diagram: % https://q.uiver.app/#q=WzAsNCxbMCwwLCJcXEdhbW1hIl0sWzAsMiwiXFxHYW1tYS5BIl0sWzIsMCwiXFxHYW1tYSJdLFsyLDIsIlxcR2FtbWEuQSJdLFsxLDMsIjFfXFxHYW1tYS5BPTFfe1xcR2FtbWEuQX0iXSxbMCwyLCIxX1xcR2FtbWEiXSxbMiwzLCJhIiwxXSxbMCwxLCJhWzFfe1xcR2FtbWF9XSIsMV1d
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Gamma && \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Gamma.A} && {\Gamma.A}
	\arrow["{1_\Gamma}", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{a[1_{\Gamma}]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["a"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{1_\Gamma.A=1_{\Gamma.A}}", from=3-1, to=3-3]
\end{tikzcd}\] commute, hence $a[1_\Gamma]=a$.

If we are given morphisms of contexts $\Omega \xrightarrow{g} \Delta \xrightarrow{f} \Gamma$ then the minimal squares in the diagram: % https://q.uiver.app/#q=WzAsNixbMiwwLCJcXERlbHRhIl0sWzIsMiwiXFxEZWx0YS5BW2ZdIl0sWzQsMCwiXFxHYW1tYSJdLFs0LDIsIlxcR2FtbWEuQSJdLFswLDIsIlxcT21lZ2EuQVtmXVtnXSJdLFswLDAsIlxcT21lZ2EiXSxbMSwzLCJmLkEiXSxbMCwyLCJmIl0sWzIsMywiYSIsMV0sWzAsMSwiYVtmXSIsMV0sWzUsNCwiYVtmXVtnXSIsMV0sWzUsMCwiZyJdLFs0LDEsImcuQVtmXSJdXQ==
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Omega && \Delta && \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Omega.A[f][g]} && {\Delta.A[f]} && {\Gamma.A}
	\arrow["g", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{a[f][g]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["f", from=1-3, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{a[f]}"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["a"{description}, from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{g.A[f]}", from=3-1, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{f.A}", from=3-3, to=3-5]
\end{tikzcd}\] commute, hence the outer rectangle---whose lower side is $(f.A)(g.A[f])=(fg).A$---commutes as well. As $a[fg]$ is the unique term of $A[fg]=A[f][g]$ making the outer rectangle commute, we conclude that $a[f][g]=a[fg]$. \endproof

\label{functoriality of a}
For a semantic term $a$, the semantic term $a[f]$ is functorial in $f$.


\proof The term $a[1_\Gamma]$ of $A[1_\Gamma]=A$ makes the diagram: % https://q.uiver.app/#q=WzAsNCxbMCwwLCJcXEdhbW1hIl0sWzAsMiwiXFxHYW1tYS5BIl0sWzIsMCwiXFxHYW1tYSJdLFsyLDIsIlxcR2FtbWEuQSJdLFsxLDMsIjFfXFxHYW1tYS5BPTFfe1xcR2FtbWEuQX0iXSxbMCwyLCIxX1xcR2FtbWEiXSxbMiwzLCJhIiwxXSxbMCwxLCJhWzFfe1xcR2FtbWF9XSIsMV1d
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Gamma && \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Gamma.A} && {\Gamma.A}
	\arrow["{1_\Gamma}", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{a[1_{\Gamma}]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["a"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{1_\Gamma.A=1_{\Gamma.A}}", from=3-1, to=3-3]
\end{tikzcd}\] commute, hence $a[1_\Gamma]=a$.

If we are given morphisms of contexts $\Omega \xrightarrow{g} \Delta \xrightarrow{f} \Gamma$ then the minimal squares in the diagram: % https://q.uiver.app/#q=WzAsNixbMiwwLCJcXERlbHRhIl0sWzIsMiwiXFxEZWx0YS5BW2ZdIl0sWzQsMCwiXFxHYW1tYSJdLFs0LDIsIlxcR2FtbWEuQSJdLFswLDIsIlxcT21lZ2EuQVtmXVtnXSJdLFswLDAsIlxcT21lZ2EiXSxbMSwzLCJmLkEiXSxbMCwyLCJmIl0sWzIsMywiYSIsMV0sWzAsMSwiYVtmXSIsMV0sWzUsNCwiYVtmXVtnXSIsMV0sWzUsMCwiZyJdLFs0LDEsImcuQVtmXSJdXQ==
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Omega && \Delta && \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Omega.A[f][g]} && {\Delta.A[f]} && {\Gamma.A}
	\arrow["g", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{a[f][g]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["f", from=1-3, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{a[f]}"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["a"{description}, from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{g.A[f]}", from=3-1, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{f.A}", from=3-3, to=3-5]
\end{tikzcd}\] commute, hence the outer rectangle---whose lower side is $(f.A)(g.A[f])=(fg).A$---commutes as well. As $a[fg]$ is the unique term of $A[fg]=A[f][g]$ making the outer rectangle commute, we conclude that $a[f][g]=a[fg]$. \endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{encode two terms in one morphism}
Suppose that $a$ and $b$ are semantic terms of some semantic type $A$ in some semantic context $\Gamma$. Then: $$a;b:=(\;\Gamma\xrightarrow{b}\Gamma.A \xrightarrow{a.A[P_A]}\Gamma.A.A[P_A]\;)=(\;\Gamma\xrightarrow{a}\Gamma.A\xrightarrow{b[P_A]}\Gamma.A.A[P_A]\;)$$ and $a;b$ is the unique arrow $\Gamma\to\Gamma.A.A[P_A]$ whose postcompositions via $P_{A[P_A]}$ and via $P_A.A$ are $a$ and $b$ respectively. Moreover, if $\Delta \xrightarrow{f}\Gamma$ is a morphism of contexts, then: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Delta &&& \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Delta.A[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]} & {\Delta.A[f].A[P_A(f.A)]} && {\Gamma.A.A[P_A]}
	\arrow["f", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{a[f];b[f]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{a;b}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{f.A.A[P_A]}", from=3-2, to=3-4]
	\arrow[Rightarrow, no head, from=3-1, to=3-2]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes.


\proof By building the term $b=b[P_Aa]$ according to the definition of $b[P_Aa]$ itself:
\[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Gamma && {\Gamma.A} && \Gamma \\
	\\
	{\Gamma.A=\Gamma.A[P_Aa]} && {\Gamma.A.A[P_A]} && {\Gamma.A} \\
	{} \\
	\Gamma && {\Gamma.A} && \Gamma
	\arrow["a", from=5-1, to=5-3]
	\arrow["{P_A}", from=5-3, to=5-5]
	\arrow["a", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{P_A}", from=1-3, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{P_A}"{description}, from=3-5, to=5-5]
	\arrow["{P_A=P_{A[P_Aa]}}"{description}, from=3-1, to=5-1]
	\arrow["b"{description}, from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{b[P_A]}"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{b[P_Aa]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{a.A[P_A]}", from=3-1, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{P_A.A}", from=3-3, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{P_{A[P_A]}}"{description}, from=3-3, to=5-3]
	\arrow["\lrcorner"{anchor=center, pos=0.125}, draw=none, from=3-3, to=5-5]
	\arrow["\lrcorner"{anchor=center, pos=0.125}, draw=none, from=3-1, to=5-3]
\end{tikzcd}\] the upper left-hand square commutes, hence we are done with the first equality. Since $P_{A[P_A]}b[P_A]a=a$ and $(P_A.A)(a.A[P_A])b=((P_Aa).A)b=b$, we conclude that $a;b$ is the unique arrow $\Gamma\to\Gamma.A.A[P_A]$ whose postcompositions via $P_{A[P_A]}$ and via $P_A.A$ are $a$ and $b$ respectively.

In order to verify that the diagram of the statement commutes, we use the first presentation: $$\Delta\xrightarrow{b[f]}\Delta.A[f]\xrightarrow{a[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]}\Delta.A[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]$$ for $a[f];b[f]$, and the second one: $$\Gamma\xrightarrow{a}\Gamma.A\xrightarrow{b[P_A]}\Gamma.A.A[P_A]$$ for $a;b$. We are left to verify that: \[\begin{tikzcd}[column sep=scriptsize]
	\Delta &&&& \Gamma && {\Gamma.A} \\
	\\
	{\Delta.A[f]} &&& {\Delta.A[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]} & {\Delta.A[f].A[P_A(f.A)]} && {\Gamma.A.A[P_A]}
	\arrow["{b[P_A]}"{description}, from=1-7, to=3-7]
	\arrow["{f.A.A[P_A]}", from=3-5, to=3-7]
	\arrow[Rightarrow, no head, from=3-4, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{b[P_A(af)]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{a[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow["f", from=1-1, to=1-5]
	\arrow["a", from=1-5, to=1-7]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes, since $b[P_A(af)]=b[f]$. Since the diagram: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\Delta &&& \Gamma &&& {\Gamma.A} \\
	\\
	{\Delta.A[f]} &&& {\Gamma.A} &&& {\Gamma.A.A[P_A]}
	\arrow["{b[P_A]}"{description}, from=1-7, to=3-7]
	\arrow["{b[P_A(af)]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["f", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["a", from=1-4, to=1-7]
	\arrow["{f.A}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{a.A[P_A]}", from=3-4, to=3-7]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes (and this is true because the equality $(a.A[P_A])(f.A)=(af).A[P_A]$ holds) we are done if we verify that: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{\Delta.A[f]} &&& {\Gamma.A} \\
	\\
	{\Delta.A[f].A[P_A(f.A)]} &&& {\Gamma.A.A[P_A]}
	\arrow["{a.A[P_A]}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{f.A}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{f.A.A[P_A]}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{a[f].A[P_A(f.A)]}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes and use that $a[f].A[f][P_{A[f]}]=a[f].A[P_A(f.A)]$. This is actually true as: $$(\;f.A.A[P_A]\;)(\;a[f].A[P_A(f.A)]\;)=(\;(f.A)a[f]\;).A[P_A]=(\;af\;).A[P_A]=(\;a.A[P_A]\;)(\;f.A\;)$$ hence we are done.
\endproof

Then: $$a;b:=(\;\Gamma\xrightarrow{b}\Gamma.A \xrightarrow{a.A[P_A]}\Gamma.A.A[P_A]\;)=(\;\Gamma\xrightarrow{a}\Gamma.A\xrightarrow{b[P_A]}\Gamma.A.A[P_A]\;)$$ and $a;b$ is the unique arrow $\Gamma\to\Gamma.A.A[P_A]$ whose postcompositions via $P_{A[P_A]}$ and via $P_A.A$ are $a$ and $b$ respectively. If we are given types: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}\text{ and }\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$$ together with a homotopy equivalence: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ then: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma : \Gamma, x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1})(\gamma,x):\Delta,A'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta : \Delta, \underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta,\underline{x}):\Gamma,A$\end{center} is a context homotopy equivalence $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$, where: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1}(\gamma,x)\equiv f(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})\equiv g(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x}):A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta))$. Conclusion: \end{center} We call $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ the \textnormal{extension of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f;g)$}.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context $\gamma_1 : \Gamma_1,\gamma_2: \Gamma_2(\gamma_1),...,\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma_1,...,\gamma_{n-1})$ and let $\delta:\Delta$ be a context $\delta_1 : \Delta_1,\delta_2: \Delta_2(\delta_1),...,\delta_m:\Delta_m(\delta_1,...,\delta_{m-1})$. If there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ then $n=m$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then $n=0=m$ and we are done. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} for some canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ and some canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$, then by inductive hypothesis $n-1=m-1$ hence we are done.
\endproof

[Reflexivity]\label{there are context identities}
Let $\gamma : \Gamma$ be a context: $$\gamma_1 : \Gamma_1,\gamma_2: \Gamma_2(\gamma_1),...,\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma_1,...,\gamma_{n-1}).$$ Then there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ from $\gamma$ to $\gamma$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\gamma)=\gamma$\,).

\proof
By induction on the length $n$ of $\gamma:\Gamma$. If $n=0$ then we are done by \texttt{(1)}. Otherwise, let $\gamma':\Gamma'$ be the context $\gamma_1,\gamma_2,...,\gamma_{n-1}$. By inductive hypothesis, there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma'$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma'):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\gamma'$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma')=\gamma'$\,).

Let us pretend that we know that there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\Gamma_n(\gamma')$ and itself relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ and such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma',\gamma_n)=\alpha(\gamma')^*\gamma_n$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\gamma_n\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\gamma_n)=\gamma_n$\,). Then the extension: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma' : \Gamma',  \gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1})(\gamma',\gamma_n):\Gamma',\Gamma_n$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma' : \Gamma',  \gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\gamma',\gamma_n):\Gamma',\Gamma_n$\end{center} of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) satisfies $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1})(\gamma)=\gamma$ (\,hence $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\gamma)=\gamma$\,). Moreover, it is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ by \texttt{(2)}.

Hence we are done if there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\Gamma_n(\gamma')$ and itself relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ and such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma',\gamma_n)=\alpha(\gamma')^*\gamma_n$. But this is true because of the following \autoref{there are identities}.
\endproof

If $n=0$ then we are done by \texttt{(1)}. Otherwise, let $\gamma':\Gamma'$ be the context $\gamma_1,\gamma_2,...,\gamma_{n-1}$. By inductive hypothesis, there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma'$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma'):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\gamma'$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma')=\gamma'$\,). Let us pretend that we know that there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\Gamma_n(\gamma')$ and itself relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ and such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma',\gamma_n)=\alpha(\gamma')^*\gamma_n$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\gamma_n\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\gamma_n)=\gamma_n$\,). entries.lean_statement:
theorem there_are_identities {Γ : Type*} {gamma : Γ} (f g : gamma → gamma) (alpha : f gamma = gamma) (S : Type*) :
    ∃ phi psi : S → S, IsCanonicalHomotopyEquiv phi psi (f, g) ∧ ∀ s, phi s = alpha s

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{there are identities}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ( and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ). Moreover, let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$. Then there exists a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and such that: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*s)=s$\,). \end{center}

\proof
By induction on the complexity of the type $S(\gamma)$.

If $S(\gamma)\equiv S$ then by \texttt{(a)} the pair: $$(\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S;\;\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S)$$ is a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. Moreover, let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$$---by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$---hence we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z:\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)=z.$$ Let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x))=\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by multiple (generalised) path induction on $(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))$. Hence, by propositional functoriality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x))=\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by substitution into the judgement: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ Finally, by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)\equiv \lambda x.g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=\lambda x. \mathsf{ev}(z,x)=z$$ and we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$, hence we are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma, \pi_1u,\pi_2u)\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u.$$ As $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u):f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u$ and: $$f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u))^*\pi_2u=\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)^*(\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u)$$---where the last equality follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)$---it is in fact the case that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u,\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*\langle \pi_1u,\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u$$ where the second equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and the third by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$. For $i=1,2$ let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,s_i(\gamma)):f(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))=\alpha(\gamma)^*s_i(\gamma)\textnormal{ and }\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_i(\alpha(\gamma)):s_i(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=\alpha(\gamma)^*s_i(\gamma)$$ hence: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;r_i(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))\bullet s_i(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1} :f(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))=s_i(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$$ and then the homotopy equivalence $(f^=,g^=)$ of \autoref{id equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. We are left to show that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma, p:s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^=(\gamma,p)=\alpha(\gamma)^*p$. In fact: $$\begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^=(\gamma,p)&=s_1(\alpha(\gamma))\bullet \alpha_1(\gamma,s_1(\gamma))^{-1}\bullet f(\gamma,p)\bullet\alpha_1(\gamma,s_2(\gamma))\bullet s_2(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\\&=s_1(\alpha(\gamma))\bullet\alpha(\gamma)^*p\bullet s_2(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\\&=\alpha(\gamma)^*p\end{aligned}$$ by propositional groupoidality and since $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$. Here the last of these equalities follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and by propositional groupoidality.
\endproof

extracted_assumptions: \label{there are identities} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ( and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ).
Moreover, let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$.

Moreover, let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$$---by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$---hence we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$.

We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z:\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)=z.$$ Let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x))=\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by multiple (generalised) path induction on $(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))$.

Hence, by propositional functoriality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x))=\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by substitution into the judgement: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ Finally, by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)\equiv \lambda x.g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=\lambda x.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$, hence we are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma, \pi_1u,\pi_2u)\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u.$$ As $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u):f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u$ and: $$f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u))^*\pi_2u=\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)^*(\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u)$$---where the last equality follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)$---it is in fact the case that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u,\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*\langle \pi_1u,\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u$$ where the second equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and the third by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
\label{there are identities}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ( and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ). Moreover, let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$. Then there exists a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and such that: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*s)=s$\,). \end{center}

\proof
By induction on the complexity of the type $S(\gamma)$.

If $S(\gamma)\equiv S$ then by \texttt{(a)} the pair: $$(\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S;\;\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S)$$ is a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. Moreover, let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$$---by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$---hence we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z:\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)=z.$$ Let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x))=\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by multiple (generalised) path induction on $(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))$. Hence, by propositional functoriality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x))=\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by substitution into the judgement: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ Finally, by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)\equiv \lambda x.g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=\lambda x. \mathsf{ev}(z,x)=z$$ and we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$, hence we are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma, \pi_1u,\pi_2u)\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u.$$ As $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u):f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u$ and: $$f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u))^*\pi_2u=\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)^*(\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u)$$---where the last equality follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)$---it is in fact the case that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u,\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*\langle \pi_1u,\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u$$ where the second equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and the third by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$. For $i=1,2$ let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,s_i(\gamma)):f(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))=\alpha(\gamma)^*s_i(\gamma)\textnormal{ and }\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_i(\alpha(\gamma)):s_i(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=\alpha(\gamma)^*s_i(\gamma)$$ hence: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;r_i(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))\bullet s_i(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1} :f(\gamma,s_i(\gamma))=s_i(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$$ and then the homotopy equivalence $(f^=,g^=)$ of \autoref{id equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. We are left to show that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma, p:s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^=(\gamma,p)=\alpha(\gamma)^*p$. In fact: $$\begin{aligned}\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^=(\gamma,p)&=s_1(\alpha(\gamma))\bullet \alpha_1(\gamma,s_1(\gamma))^{-1}\bullet f(\gamma,p)\bullet\alpha_1(\gamma,s_2(\gamma))\bullet s_2(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\\&=s_1(\alpha(\gamma))\bullet\alpha(\gamma)^*p\bullet s_2(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\\&=\alpha(\gamma)^*p\end{aligned}$$ by propositional groupoidality and since $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$. Here the last of these equalities follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and by propositional groupoidality.
\endproof
context_pack.section: Properties of the family of canonical equivalences
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{there are identities} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ( and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ). Moreover, let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$. Then there exists a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and such that: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*s)=s$\,). \end{center} \proof By induction on the complexity of the type $S(\gamma)$. If $S(\gamma)\equiv S$ then by \texttt{(a)} the pair: $$(\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S;\;\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,s)\equiv s:S)$$ is a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. Moreover, let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$$---by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$---hence we are done. If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x
definitions: let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$.

assumptions: \label{there are identities} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ( and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ ).
Moreover, let us assume that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$.

Moreover, let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)\equiv s=\alpha(\gamma)^*s$$---by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$---hence we are done.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$.

We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z:\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)=z.$$ Let us observe that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x))=\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by multiple (generalised) path induction on $(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))$.

Hence, by propositional functoriality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(z,x))=\mathsf{ev}(z,x)$$ where the second equality holds by substitution into the judgement: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ Finally, by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,z\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*z)\equiv \lambda x.g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*z,f_1(\gamma,x)))=\lambda x.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and itself relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y$ and equivalently: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*y)=y.$$ The homotopy equivalence $(f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is a canonical homotopy equivalence relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$, hence we are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma, \pi_1u,\pi_2u)\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u.$$ As $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u):f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u$ and: $$f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u))^*\pi_2u=\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)^*(\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u)$$---where the last equality follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,\pi_1u)$---it is in fact the case that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_1u,\alpha(\gamma)^*\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*\langle \pi_1u,\pi_2u\rangle=\alpha(\gamma)^*u$$ where the second equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$ and the third by propositional functoriality and propositional $\eta$-expansion.

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$, then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma,x):A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma,x'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*x)=x$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Symmetry]\label{symmetry}
If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$, then there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;\rangle$ between $\delta$ and $\gamma$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then we are done. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} for some canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma':\Gamma'$ and $\delta':\Delta'$ and some canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma')$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ \begin{center}---hence $\gamma\equiv\gamma',x:A(\gamma') $ and $\delta\equiv \delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta')$---\end{center} then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;\rangle$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma')$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\delta'):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta')=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta')$. Moreover, by the following \autoref{symmetry of canonical equiv} there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;g'\;|\;f'\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\delta',\underline{x}):A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta'))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\delta',x'):A'(\delta')$\end{center} relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}';\textnormal{\texttt{f}}')$ such that: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\delta',\underline{x}):g(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta')^*\underline{x})=\alpha(\delta')^*g'(\delta',\underline{x})$\\ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'),\alpha(\delta')^*x')=\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta')^*f'(\delta',x')$\,)\end{center} hence by \texttt{(2)} the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1};\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1})$ of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;g'\;|\;f'\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;\rangle$ between $\delta',\underline{x}\equiv \delta$ and $\gamma',x\equiv \gamma$ and: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\alpha(\delta'),\alpha_1(\delta',\underline{x})):(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})$$ that is: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)\equiv(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta)=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta)\equiv \textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$$ hence we are done. \endproof

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
[Symmetry]\label{symmetry}
If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$, then there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;\rangle$ between $\delta$ and $\gamma$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then we are done. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} for some canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma':\Gamma'$ and $\delta':\Delta'$ and some canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma')$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ \begin{center}---hence $\gamma\equiv\gamma',x:A(\gamma') $ and $\delta\equiv \delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta')$---\end{center} then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;\rangle$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma')$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\delta'):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta')=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta')$. Moreover, by the following \autoref{symmetry of canonical equiv} there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;g'\;|\;f'\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\delta',\underline{x}):A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta'))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\delta',x'):A'(\delta')$\end{center} relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}';\textnormal{\texttt{f}}')$ such that: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\delta',\underline{x}):g(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta')^*\underline{x})=\alpha(\delta')^*g'(\delta',\underline{x})$\\ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta',x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'),\alpha(\delta')^*x')=\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta')^*f'(\delta',x')$\,)\end{center} hence by \texttt{(2)} the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1};\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1})$ of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;g'\;|\;f'\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;\rangle$ between $\delta',\underline{x}\equiv \delta$ and $\gamma',x\equiv \gamma$ and: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\alpha(\delta'),\alpha_1(\delta',\underline{x})):(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})$$ that is: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)\equiv(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta)=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta)\equiv \textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$$ hence we are done. \endproof

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop}[Symmetry]\label{symmetry} If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$, then there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;\rangle$ between $\delta$ and $\gamma$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$. \proof By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then we are done. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} for some canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma':\Gamma'$ and $\delta':\Delta'$ and some canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ between $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma')$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ \begin{center}---hence $\gamma\equiv\gamma',x:A(\gamma') $ and $\delta\equiv \delta',\underline{x}:A'(\delta')$---\end{center} then by inductive hypothesis there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;\rangle$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}

entries.lean_statement: theorem symmetry_of_canonical_equiv {Γ delta : Type*} [Category Γ] [Category delta] (gamma : Γ) (delta : delta) (f : Γ ⥤ delta) (g : delta ⥤ Γ) (f' : delta ⥤ Γ) (g' : Γ ⥤ delta) (alpha : g ⋙ f = g' ⋙ f') (S : Γ ⥤ Type*) (T : delta ⥤ Type*) (phi : (gamma : Γ) → (x : S gamma) → T (f gamma)) (psi : (gamma : Γ) → (x' : T (f gamma)) → S gamma) (hphipsi : IsCanonicalHomotopyEquiv (f; g) phi psi) : ∃ (phi' : (delta : delta) → (x : T delta) → S (g' delta)) (psi' : (delta : delta) → (x' : S (g' delta)) → T delta), IsCanonicalHomotopyEquiv (g'; f') phi' psi' ∧ (∀ delta x, psi (g delta) (q delta ^ x) = alpha ^ (psi' delta x)) ∧ (∀ delta x', phi (g delta) (alpha ^ x') = q delta ^ (phi' delta x'))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: \label{symmetry of canonical equiv} Suppose that we are given a canonical homotopy equivalence $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ between $\gamma: \Gamma$ and $\delta: \Delta$ and suppose that there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}';\textnormal{\texttt{f}}')$ between $\delta$ and $\gamma$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\delta):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta)$.; If we are given judgements: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ \end{center} and if there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,x):T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\underline{x}'):S(\gamma)$\end{center} between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to a context homotopy equivalence $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$, then there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\psi'\;|\;\phi'\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:T(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi'(\delta,\underline{x}):S(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,x':S(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi'(\delta,x'):T(\delta)$\end{center} relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}';\textnormal{\texttt{f}}')$ such that: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:T(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})=\alpha(\delta)^*\psi'(\delta,\underline{x})$\\and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,x':S(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\alpha(\delta)^*x')=\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\phi'(\delta,x')$.\end{center} \proof By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.; \medskip If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(b)} then $S(\gamma)\equiv \Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ and $T(\delta)\equiv \Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ for some judgements: \begin{itemize} \item $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ \item $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B'(\delta,\underline{x}):\textsc{Type}$ \end{itemize} and there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), in such a way that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.; $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta),g_1'(\delta,\underline{x}))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*y')=(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}))^*f'_2(\delta,\underline{x},y')$\end{center} where we remind that: \[\begin{aligned} \textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})&\equiv g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})\\ \textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})&\equiv q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})^{-1} \end{aligned}\] in context $\delta,\underline{x}$, being $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;q_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):\underline{x}'=f_1(\gamma,g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'))$.; We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,z':\Pi_{x':A(g'(\delta))}B(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta),x')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Pi(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\alpha(\delta)^*z')=\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*f'^\Pi(\delta,z').$$ Let us observe that $f'^\Pi(\delta,z')\equiv$ \[\begin{aligned} &\equiv \lambda \underline{x}:A'(\delta).f_2'(\delta,\underline{x},\mathsf{ev}(z',g_1'(\delta,\underline{x})))\\ &=\lambda \underline{x}.(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^{-1})^*q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})^*f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*\mathsf{ev}(z',g_1'(\delta,\underline{x})))\\ &=\lambda \underline{x}.(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^{-1})^*q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})^*f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\delta)^*z',g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),q(\delta)^*\underline{x}))) \end{aligned}\] in context $\delta,z'$, where the first propositional equality follows by: \begin{center}$\judge{\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta),g_1'(\delta,\underline{x}))}$\\ ${f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*y')=(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}))^*f'_2(\delta,\underline{x},y')}$\end{center} and the second by: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}):g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})=\alpha(\delta)^*g'_1(\delta,\underline{x}).$$ Therefore $q(\delta)^*f'^\Pi(\delta,z')=$ $$\begin{aligned}\text{ }&=q(\delta)^*\lambda \underline{x}.(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^{-1})^*q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})^*f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\delta)^*z',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})))\\&=\lambda \underline{x}'':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta))).q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\underline{x}'')^*f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\underline{x}''),\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\delta)^*z',g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\underline{x}'')))\\&\equiv f^\Pi(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\alpha(\delta)^*z')\end{aligned}$$ in context ${\delta,z'}$, where the first propositional equality follows by propositional function extensionality and propositional functoriality and the second by multiple (generalised) path induction on $q(\delta)$.; \medskip If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(c)} then as before $S(\gamma)\equiv \Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ and $T(\delta)\equiv \Sigma_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ for some judgements: \begin{itemize} \item $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ \item $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B'(\delta,\underline{x}):\textsc{Type}$ \end{itemize} and there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), in such a way that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.; We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}:\Sigma_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Sigma(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{u})=\alpha(\delta)^*g'^\Sigma(\delta,\underline{u}).$$ Let us observe that: \[\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})&=\alpha(\delta)^*g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})\\ \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*\pi_2\underline{u})&=(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\\ &=\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})^*\alpha(\delta)^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u}) \end{aligned}\] where the first equality in the second judgement follows by: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta),\underline{y}:B'(\delta,\underline{x})\boldsymbol{\rfloor}$\\ ${g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}))^*\underline{y})=(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*g'_1(\delta,\underline{x},\underline{y})}$ \end{center} and the second by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})$.; Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} \langle \textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),& g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*\pi_2\underline{u}) \rangle\\=&\langle \alpha(\delta)^*g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}), \alpha(\delta)^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\rangle\\=&\alpha(\delta)^*\langle g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}), g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\rangle\\\equiv& \alpha(\delta)^*g'^\Sigma(\delta,\underline{u}) \end{aligned}$$ in context ${\delta,\underline{u}}$, where the second propositional equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\delta)$. Conclusion: \medskip If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(a)} then we are done. By inductive hypothesis there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;g'_1\;|\;f'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\delta,\underline{x}):A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\delta,x'):A'(\delta)$\end{center} between $A

\medskip If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(b)} then $S(\gamma)\equiv \Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ and $T(\delta)\equiv \Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ for some judgements: \begin{itemize} \item $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ \, and \,
