$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B'(\delta,\underline{x}):\textsc{Type}$ \end{itemize} and there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.

$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta),y':B(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta),g_1'(\delta,\underline{x}))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*y')=(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}))^*f'_2(\delta,\underline{x},y')$\end{center} where we remind that: \[\begin{aligned} \textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})&\equiv g_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})\\ \textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x})&\equiv q_1(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{x})^{-1} \end{aligned}\] in context $\delta,\underline{x}$, being $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;q_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):\underline{x}'=f_1(\gamma,g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'))$.

We are left to verify that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}:\Sigma_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Sigma(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta)^*\underline{u})=\alpha(\delta)^*g'^\Sigma(\delta,\underline{u}).$$ Let us observe that: \[\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})&=\alpha(\delta)^*g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})\\ \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,\underline{u}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*\pi_2\underline{u})&=(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\\ &=\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})^*\alpha(\delta)^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u}) \end{aligned}\] where the first equality in the second judgement follows by: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta,\underline{x}:A'(\delta),\underline{y}:B'(\delta,\underline{x})\boldsymbol{\rfloor}$\\ ${g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\underline{x}))^*\underline{y})=(\alpha(\delta),\alpha_1(\delta,\underline{x}))^*g'_1(\delta,\underline{x},\underline{y})}$ \end{center} and the second by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u})$.

Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} \langle \textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),& g_2(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}),(\textnormal{\texttt{q}}(\delta),\textnormal{\texttt{q}}_{n+1}(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}))^*\pi_2\underline{u}) \rangle\\=&\langle \alpha(\delta)^*g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}), \alpha(\delta)^*g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\rangle\\=&\alpha(\delta)^*\langle g_1'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u}), g_2'(\delta,\pi_1\underline{u},\pi_2\underline{u})\rangle\\\equiv& \alpha(\delta)^*g'^\Sigma(\delta,\underline{u}) \end{aligned}$$ in context ${\delta,\underline{u}}$, where the second propositional equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\delta)$.

entries.lean_statement: theorem lemma_x (gamma : Γ) (delta : delta) (omega : Ω) (f : delta → Γ) (g : Γ → delta) (f' : Ω → delta) (g' : delta → Ω) (S : Γ → Type) (T : delta → Type) (U : Ω → Type) (phi : Π gamma, S gamma → T (f gamma)) (psi : Π gamma, T (g gamma) → S gamma) (phi' : Π delta, T delta → U (f' delta)) (psi' : Π delta, U (g' delta) → T delta) (hphi : IsCanonicalHomotopyEquiv (S gamma) (T (f gamma)) phi psi) (hphi' : IsHomotopyEquiv (T delta) (U (f' delta)) phi' psi') : ∃ phi'' psi'', IsCanonicalHomotopyEquiv (S gamma) (U (f' (f gamma))) phi'' psi'' ∧ (∀ gamma s, phi'' gamma s = phi' (f gamma) (phi gamma s)) ∧ (∀ gamma u'', psi'' gamma u'' = psi gamma (psi' (f gamma) u''))

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{lemma x} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$, $\delta:\Delta$ and $\omega:\Omega$ be contexts, let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\delta$ and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\delta$ and $\omega$. If we are given judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$, $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\omega\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;U(\omega):\textsc{Type}$ and canonical homotopy equivalences $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ between $T(\delta)$ and $U(\omega)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$, then there exists a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;{\phi}''\;|\;{\psi}''\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and $U(\omega)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and such that: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;{\phi}''(\gamma,s)=\phi'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),\phi(\gamma,s))$\\ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u'':U(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;{\psi}''(\gamma,u'')=\psi(\gamma,\psi'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),u''))$. \end{center} \proof By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. \endproof \end{lem}
definitions: let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\delta$ and $\omega$.

theorem lemma_y
    (gamma : Γ) (delta : delta)
    (f g : ContextHomotopyEquiv gamma delta) (f' g' : ContextHomotopyEquiv gamma delta)
    (alpha : f.1 gamma = f'.1 gamma) (beta : g.1 delta = g'.1 delta)
    (S : gamma → Type) (T : delta → Type)
    (phi : S gamma → T delta) (psi : (gamma : Γ) → (t : T (f.1 gamma)) → T (f'.1 gamma))
    (hphipsi : IsCanonicalHEquiv phi psi (f.1, g.1)) :
    ∃ phi' psi',
      IsCanonicalHEquiv phi' psi' (f'.1, g'.1) ∧
      (∀ s, phi gamma s = alpha ▸ (phi' gamma s)) ∧
      (∀ t', psi gamma (alpha ▸ t') = psi' gamma t')

theorem transitivity {Γ delta Ω : Type*} (gamma : Γ) (delta : delta) (omega : Ω)
    (c : ContextHomotopyEquiv gamma delta) (c' : ContextHomotopyEquiv delta omega) :
    ∃ c'' : ContextHomotopyEquiv gamma omega,
      (⌊gamma⌋ c'' gamma = c' (c gamma)) ∧ (⌊delta⌋ (d'' delta) = d (d' delta))

If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\delta$ and $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle$ is a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\delta$ and $\omega$ then there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}''\;\rangle$ between $\gamma$ and $\omega$ such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}''(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta))$. \proof By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. theorem lemma_z (gamma : Γ) (gamma' : Γ') (delta : delta) (f g : ContextHomotopy gamma gamma') (a : gamma → delta) (a' : gamma' → delta) (alpha : gamma → gamma') (h : ∀ x, a x = a' (alpha x)) :
    ∀ {S : delta → Type}, (∀ x, S x) → ∃ (ϕ : gamma → S (a gamma)) (psi : gamma' → S (a' gamma')),
      (∀ x, alpha x ▸ ϕ x = x) ∧ (∀ x', psi x' = alpha (f x') ▸ x')

semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: %[Il Lemma del Destino] \label{lemma z} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$, $\gamma':\Gamma'$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts, let $( \textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\gamma'$ and let $a(\gamma),a'(\gamma'):\Delta$ be context morphisms $\gamma\to\delta$ and $\gamma'\to\delta$ respectively such that: \[\begin{tikzcd} \gamma \\ & \delta% & \textnormal{E \, \, S \, \, T \, \, I \, \, N \, \, Y} \\ {\gamma'} \arrow[""{name=0, anchor=center, inner sep=0}, "{a(\gamma)}", bend left, from=1-1, to=2-2] \arrow["{a'(\gamma')}"', bend right, from=3-1, to=2-2] \arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)}"', from=1-1, to=3-1] \arrow["{\alpha(\gamma)}"{description}, shorten <=15pt, shorten >=15pt, Rightarrow, from=0, to=3-1] \end{tikzcd}\] Then, whenever $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\delta):\textsc{Type}$, there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(a(\gamma))$ and $S(a'(\gamma'))$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ such that:\begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:S(a(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi(\gamma,x)=x$ \;\;\; Conclusion: and equivalently \;\;\; $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{x}':S(a'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\underline{x}')=\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{x}'$. \end{center} \proof By induction on the complexity of $S(\delta)$. \endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{semi destiny}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\gamma':\Gamma'$ be contexts and let $( \textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\gamma'$. Then, whenever $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}$, there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ and $S(\gamma')$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and such that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:S(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,x)=\psi(\gamma,x)=x.$$

\proof
Follows by \autoref{lemma z}, where $\delta \equiv \gamma'$, $a(\gamma)\equiv \textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)$, $a'(\gamma')\equiv \gamma'$ and $\alpha(\gamma)\equiv \mathsf{r}(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$.
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{lemma w}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\gamma':\Gamma'$ be contexts and let $( \textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\gamma'$. Suppose that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}$ are canonically homotopy equivalent via some $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. Let $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ be some canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is pairwise propositionally equal to $( \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma;\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma)$ (whose existence is ensured by \autoref{there are context identities}) and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\gamma$.


Then $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ are canonically homotopy equivalent via some $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ in such a way that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi'(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi(\gamma,x)$$ and equivalently $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{x}':T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi'(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{x}')=\psi(\gamma,\underline{x}')$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.
\endproof

\label{small reindexing}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts, let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $\delta$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\delta):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\delta)=\delta$ and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha'(\gamma):\gamma=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)$. Moreover, let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;a(\gamma):\Delta$ be a context morphism and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ be such that there is a canonical   homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\delta)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. Then there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ between $S(a(\gamma))$ and $T(a(\gamma))$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and such that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:S(a(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(a(\gamma),x)=\alpha''(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,x)$$ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{x}:T(a(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(a(\gamma),\alpha''(\gamma)^*\underline{x})=\psi'(\gamma,\underline{x})$ where $\alpha''(\gamma)\equiv \alpha(a(\gamma))\bullet a(\alpha'(\gamma))$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be a context $\gamma_1 : \Gamma_1,\gamma_2: \Gamma_2(\gamma_1),...,\gamma_n:\Gamma_n(\gamma_1,...,\gamma_{n-1})$ and let $\delta:\Delta$ be a context $\delta_1 : \Delta_1,\delta_2: \Delta_2(\delta_1),...,\delta_m:\Delta_m(\delta_1,...,\delta_{m-1})$. If there is a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ then $\gamma$ has h-propositional identities if and only if $\delta$ has h-propositional identities.

\proof
By \autoref{symmetry} we can assume w.l.o.g. that $\gamma$ has h-propositional identities and we are left to verify that $\delta$ has h-propositional identities as well. By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then we are done. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} then $\gamma  : \Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ are of the form $\gamma'  : \Gamma',x : A(\gamma')$ and $\delta' :  \Delta',\underline{x}: A'(\delta')$ respectively and there are a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'):\Delta'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'):\Gamma'$\end{center} and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma',x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\underline{x}'):A(\gamma')$\end{center} between $A(\gamma')$ and $A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$, in such a way that the extension: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma' : \Gamma',  x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1})(\gamma',x):\Delta',A'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta' : \Delta', \underline{x}:A'(\delta')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x}):\Gamma',A$\end{center} of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) is the canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. By inductive hypothesis and being $\gamma'$ a context with h-propositional identities, the context $\delta'$ has h-propositional identities. Moreover, by the following \autoref{p} and having $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}$ h-propositional identities, we deduce that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta'):\textsc{Type}$ has h-propositional identities. Therefore $\delta$ has h-propositional identities and we are done.
\endproof

Moreover, by the following \autoref{p} and having $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}$ h-propositional identities, we deduce that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta'):\textsc{Type}$ has h-propositional identities. Therefore $\delta$ has h-propositional identities and we are done.
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{p}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts and let $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ be a canonical context homotopy equivalence between $\gamma$ and $\delta$. Let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ be such that there is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$. Then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ has h-propositional identities if and only if $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ has h-propositional identities.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(a)} then we are done. If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(b)} then $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ and $T(\delta)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x':A'(\delta)}B(\delta,x')$, where $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ have h-propositional identities, $A(\gamma)$ is canonically homotopy equivalent to $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and $B(\gamma,x)$ is canonically homotopy equivalent to $B'(\delta,x')$ relative to the extension of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via the given canonical homotopy equivalence between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$. By inductive hypothesis, the judgements $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B'(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ have h-propositional identities, hence $T(\delta)$ has h-propositional identities and we are done. If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(c)} then we infer that $T(\delta)$ has h-propositional identities as for the case \texttt{(b)}. Finally, if $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(d)} then $S(\gamma)$ is an h-proposition of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_1(\gamma),s_2(\gamma):A(\gamma)$, while $T(\delta)$ is of the form $t_1(\delta)=t_2(\delta)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;t_1(\delta),t_2(\delta):A'(\delta)$, being $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ canonically homotopy equivalent relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ in such a way that the canonical homotopy equivalence propositionally identifies $s_i(\gamma)$ and $t_i(\gamma)$, for $i \in  \{1,2\}$. By inductive hypothesis, the judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ has h-propositional identities. Moreover $t_1(\delta)=t_2(\delta)$ happens to be an h-proposition, since it is homotopy equivalent (via $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$) to an h-set. Hence $T(\delta)$ has h-propositional identities and we are done.
\endproof

If $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(c)} then we infer that $T(\delta)$ has h-propositional identities as for the case \texttt{(b)}.

Finally, if $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(d)} then $S(\gamma)$ is an h-proposition of the form $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_1(\gamma),s_2(\gamma):A(\gamma)$, while $T(\delta)$ is of the form $t_1(\delta)=t_2(\delta)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;t_1(\delta),t_2(\delta):A'(\delta)$, being $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ canonically homotopy equivalent relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ in such a way that the canonical homotopy equivalence propositionally identifies $s_i(\gamma)$ and $t_i(\gamma)$, for $i \in \{1,2\}$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{only one canonical context homotopy equivalence}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts with h-propositional identities. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ and $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle$ are canonical context homotopy equivalences between $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$, then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$ (i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$).

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(1)} then $\gamma : \Gamma$ and $\delta : \Delta$ are the empty contexts hence $\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\equiv \textnormal{\texttt{c}}'$ and $\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\equiv\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is of the form \texttt{(2)} then $\gamma  : \Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ are of the form $\gamma'  : \Gamma',x : A(\gamma')$ and $\delta' :  \Delta',\underline{x}: A'(\delta')$ respectively and there are a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'):\Delta'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta'):\Gamma'$\end{center} and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ : \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma',x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\underline{x}'):A(\gamma')$\end{center} between $A(\gamma')$ and $A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$, in such a way that the extension: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma' : \Gamma',  x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{n+1})(\gamma',x):\Delta',A'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta' : \Delta', \underline{x}:A'(\delta')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x}):\Gamma',A$\end{center} of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;f\;|\;g\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) is the given $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$. Therefore $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle$ cannot be of the form \texttt{(1)} as (e.g.) $\delta:\Delta$ is not the empty context, which means that $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle$, being canonical, is of the form \texttt{(2)}. Hence, as before, there are a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma'):\Delta'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta'):\Gamma'$\end{center} and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'\;|\;g'\;\rangle$ : \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma',x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma'))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma'))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}'):A(\gamma')$\end{center} between $A(\gamma')$ and $A'(\delta')$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;\rangle$, in such a way that the extension: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma' : \Gamma',  x:A(\gamma')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{n+1})(\gamma',x):\Delta',A'$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta' : \Delta', \underline{x}:A'(\delta')\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x}):\Gamma',A$\end{center} of $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'\;\rangle$ via $\langle\;f'\;|\;g'\;\rangle$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}) is the given $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle$. Now $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma'):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma')$ (i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{\alpha}(\delta'):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta')=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta')$) by inductive hypothesis.

Let us pretend that we know that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*f'(\gamma',x)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\underline{\underline{x}}')=g'(\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}')$\,), that is: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{n+1}(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{n+1}(\gamma',x)$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta',\underline{x})=\tilde{\alpha}(\delta')^*\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1}(\delta',\underline{x})$\,).
\end{center} Then: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{n+1})(\gamma',x)=(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{n+1})(\gamma',x)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})$\,) that is: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$\,).

Hence we are done if $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*f'(\gamma',x)$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\underline{\underline{x}}')=g'(\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}')$\,), but this is actually the case by the following \autoref{only one canonical homotopy equivalence}.
\endproof

Now $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma'):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma')=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma')$ (i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta':\Delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{\alpha}(\delta'):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta')=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta')$) by inductive hypothesis.

Let us pretend that we know that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*f'(\gamma',x)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\underline{\underline{x}}')=g'(\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}')$\,), that is: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{n+1}(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{n+1}(\gamma',x)$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1}(\delta',\underline{x})=\tilde{\alpha}(\delta')^*\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1}(\delta',\underline{x})$\,).
\end{center} Then: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{n+1})(\gamma',x)=(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{n+1})(\gamma',x)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',\underline{x}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;(\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})=(\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})(\delta',\underline{x})$\,) that is: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\gamma)$$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'(\delta)$\,).

Hence we are done if $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma',x)=\alpha(\gamma')^*f'(\gamma',x)$ (\,i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\gamma',\alpha(\gamma')^*\underline{\underline{x}}')=g'(\gamma',\underline{\underline{x}}')$\,), but this is actually the case by the following \autoref{only one canonical homotopy equivalence}.
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{only one canonical homotopy equivalence}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts together with context homotopy equivalences $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ such that: \begin{center}  $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)$ (i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{\alpha}(\delta):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta)$) \end{center} and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta :\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ have h-propositional identities. Moreover, let $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and let $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$. Then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,s)$ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{t}':T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{t}')=\psi'(\gamma,\underline{t}')$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $S(\gamma)$.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is a base type $S$ then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the context $\delta$ needs to be $\gamma$ and $T(\delta)$ needs to be $S$, hence both $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ and $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ need to be of the form \texttt{(a)} and we are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Pi_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities. Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Pi;g'^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$. By inductive hypothesis: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)$ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{x}')=g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}')$ \end{center} and: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*f'_2(\gamma,x,y)$\\ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\underline{y}')=g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}')$. \end{center} Then: \[\begin{aligned}
g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',f_1(\gamma,x)))&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*(\alpha(\gamma),\mathsf{r}(\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\alpha(\gamma)^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g'_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))
\end{aligned}\] in context ${\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))}$, where the first and the fourth equalities follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,x)$ and the second and the third by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$. By propositional function extensionality, we deduce that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}')=g'^\Pi(\gamma,\underline{z}')$$ and we are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Sigma_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities. Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Sigma;g'^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$. By inductive hypothesis: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)$ \end{center} and: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*f'_2(\gamma,x,y)$
\end{center} hence: \[\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)&\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&= \alpha(\gamma)^*\langle f'_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f'_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&\equiv \alpha(\gamma)^*f'^\Sigma(\gamma,u)
\end{aligned}\] where the second propositional equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$. We are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is an h-proposition $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_1(\gamma),s_2(\gamma):A(\gamma)$ then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be an h-proposition $t_1(\delta)=t_2(\delta)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;t_1(\delta),t_2(\delta):A'(\delta)$. We observe that $\phi(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ and $\phi'(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$ in context $\gamma,p:s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$, hence $\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$. Since $t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ an h-proposition, then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,p)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,p)$ and we are done. \endproof
semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: \label{only one canonical homotopy equivalence} Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts together with context homotopy equivalences $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ such that: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)$ (i.e.; $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{\alpha}(\delta):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta)$) \end{center} and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta :\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ have h-propositional identities.; Moreover, let $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and let $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$.; Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.; Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Pi;g'^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$.; \end{center} Then: \[\begin{aligned} g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',f_1(\gamma,x)))&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*(\alpha(\gamma),\mathsf{r}(\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\alpha(\gamma)^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g'_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x))) \end{aligned}\] in context ${\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))}$, where the first and the fourth equalities follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,x)$ and the second and the third by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$.; semantic_equivalence_artifact.lean_statement: theorem only_one_canonical_homotopy_equivalence (gamma : Γ) (delta : delta) (f g : delta → Γ) (f' g' : delta → Γ) (alpha : Γ → f = f') (S : Γ → Type) (T : delta → Type) (phi psi : (gamma : Γ) → S gamma → T (f gamma)) (phi' psi' : (gamma : Γ) → S gamma → T (f' gamma)) (halpha : ∀ gamma, f gamma = f' gamma) (hS : ∀ gamma, S gamma) (hT : ∀ delta, T delta) (hphi : ∀ gamma s, phi gamma s = phi' gamma s) (hpsi : ∀ gamma t, psi gamma t = psi' gamma t) : ∀ gamma s, phi gamma s = alpha gamma ^* phi' gamma s

\medskip If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Pi_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities.

Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.

Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Pi;g'^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$.

\end{center} Then: \[\begin{aligned} g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',f_1(\gamma,x)))&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*(\alpha(\gamma),\mathsf{r}(\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\alpha(\gamma)^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\ &=g'_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x))) \end{aligned}\] in context ${\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))}$, where the first and the fourth equalities follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,x)$ and the second and the third by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$.

\medskip If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Sigma_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities.

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
\label{only one canonical homotopy equivalence}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ and $\delta:\Delta$ be contexts together with context homotopy equivalences $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ such that: \begin{center}  $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma)=\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma)$ (i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta:\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{\alpha}(\delta):\textnormal{\texttt{g}}(\delta)=\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'(\delta)$) \end{center} and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;S(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta :\Delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ have h-propositional identities. Moreover, let $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and let $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $S(\gamma)$ and $T(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$. Then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,s:S(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,s)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,s)$ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{t}':T(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\psi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{t}')=\psi'(\gamma,\underline{t}')$.

\proof
By induction on the complexity of $S(\gamma)$.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is a base type $S$ then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the context $\delta$ needs to be $\gamma$ and $T(\delta)$ needs to be $S$, hence both $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ and $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$ need to be of the form \texttt{(a)} and we are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Pi_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Pi_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities. Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Pi;g'^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$. By inductive hypothesis: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)$ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{x}')=g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}')$ \end{center} and: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*f'_2(\gamma,x,y)$\\ i.e. $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\underline{y}')=g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}')$. \end{center} Then: \[\begin{aligned}
g_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',f_1(\gamma,x)))&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\mathsf{ev}(\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}',\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*(\alpha(\gamma),\mathsf{r}(\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,\alpha_1(\gamma,x)^*\alpha(\gamma)^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g_2(\gamma,x,(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))\\
&=g'_2(\gamma,x,\mathsf{ev}(\underline{z}',f'_1(\gamma,x)))
\end{aligned}\] in context ${\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))}$, where the first and the fourth equalities follows by (generalised) path induction on $\alpha_1(\gamma,x)$ and the second and the third by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$. By propositional function extensionality, we deduce that: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,\underline{z}':[\Pi_{\underline{x}:A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,\underline{x})](\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g^\Pi(\gamma,\alpha(\gamma)^*\underline{z}')=g'^\Pi(\gamma,\underline{z}')$$ and we are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is of the form $\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\gamma,x):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be of the form $\Sigma_{x':A'(\delta)}B'(\delta,x')$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta,x'):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities. Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Sigma;g^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$. Analogously, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_1\;|\;g'_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f'_2\;|\;g'_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma),f'_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}',\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}}',\textnormal{\texttt{g}}'_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}';\textnormal{\texttt{g}}')$ via $(f'_1;g'_1)$ (\autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f'^\Sigma;g'^\Sigma)$ of \autoref{sigma equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi'\;|\;\psi'\;\rangle$. By inductive hypothesis: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):f_1(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,x)$ \end{center} and: \begin{center} $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y)=(\alpha(\gamma),\alpha_1(\gamma,x))^*f'_2(\gamma,x,y)$
\end{center} hence: \[\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,u:\Sigma_{x:A(\gamma)}B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f^\Sigma(\gamma,u)&\equiv\langle f_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&=\langle \alpha(\gamma)^*f'_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),\alpha(\gamma)^*f'_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&= \alpha(\gamma)^*\langle f'_1(\gamma,\pi_1u),f'_2(\gamma,\pi_1u,\pi_2u) \rangle\\
&\equiv \alpha(\gamma)^*f'^\Sigma(\gamma,u)
\end{aligned}\] where the second propositional equality follows by multiple (generalised) path induction on $\alpha(\gamma)$. We are done.

\medskip

If $S(\gamma)$ is an h-proposition $s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;s_1(\gamma),s_2(\gamma):A(\gamma)$ then, being $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ a canonical homotopy equivalence, the type $T(\delta)$ needs to be an h-proposition $t_1(\delta)=t_2(\delta)$ for some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A'(\delta):\textsc{Type}$ with h-propositional identities and some $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;t_1(\delta),t_2(\delta):A'(\delta)$. We observe that $\phi(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ and $\phi'(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}'(\gamma))$ in context $\gamma,p:s_1(\gamma)=s_2(\gamma)$, hence $\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,p):t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$. Since $t_1(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))=t_2(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$ an h-proposition, then $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi(\gamma,p)=\alpha(\gamma)^*\phi'(\gamma,p)$ and we are done. \endproof

Moreover, there are a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_1\;|\;g_1\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_1(\gamma,x):A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))$\\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,\underline{x}':A'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_1(\gamma,\underline{x}'):A(\gamma)$\end{center} between $A(\gamma)$ and $A'(\delta)$ relative to $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ and a canonical homotopy equivalence $\langle\;f_2\;|\;g_2\;\rangle$: \begin{center}$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),y:B(\gamma,x)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f_2(\gamma,x,y):B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))$ \\ $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma,x:A(\gamma),\underline{y}':B'(\textnormal{\texttt{f}}(\gamma),f_1(\gamma,x))\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g_2(\gamma,x,\underline{y}'):B(\gamma,x)$\end{center} between $B(\gamma,x)$ and $B'(\delta,\underline{x})$ relative to the extension $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}},\textnormal{\texttt{f}}_{m+1};\textnormal{\texttt{g}},\textnormal{\texttt{g}}_{n+1})$ of $(\textnormal{\texttt{f}};\textnormal{\texttt{g}})$ via $(f_1;g_1)$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}), such that the homotopy equivalence $( f^\Pi;g^\Pi)$ of \autoref{pi equiv} is the given $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{semantic context cat}
    There is a category $\mathcal{C}$ such that: \begin{itemize}
        \item the objects of $\mathcal{C}$ are the equivalence classes $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ of h-elementary contexts;
        \item the arrows $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\delta:\Delta]$ of $\mathcal{C}$ are the equivalence classes $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']$ of morphisms of contexts $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta'$ where $[\gamma':\Gamma']=[\gamma]$ and $[\delta':\Delta']=[\delta]$;
        \item if we are given arrows:\begin{center} $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']:[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\delta:\Delta]$ and $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']:[\delta:\Delta]\to[\omega:\Omega]$ \end{center} then, whenever $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\delta'\to\delta''$, the composition arrow: \begin{center} $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']\circ[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']:[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\omega:\Omega]$ \end{center} is the arrow $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\gamma'))):\Omega'].$
    \end{itemize} Moreover, the category $\mathcal{C}$ has a terminal object.
\proof Observe that the composition operation is in fact well-defined: if we are given: \begin{center}$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'':\Gamma''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma''):\Delta''']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']$ and $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta^{iv}:\Delta^{iv}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\delta^{iv}):\Omega'']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']$\end{center} then: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{\gamma'} && {\delta'} && {\delta''} && {\omega'} \\
	\\
	{\gamma''} && {\delta'''} && {\delta^{iv}} && {\omega''}
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''(\delta')}"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}(\gamma')}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{f'(\gamma'')}"', from=3-1, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\delta')}", from=1-3, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\delta''')}"', from=3-3, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{f(\gamma')}", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g(\delta'')}", from=1-5, to=1-7]
	\arrow["{g'(\delta^{iv})}"', from=3-5, to=3-7]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''(\delta'')}"{description}, from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{v}(\omega')}", from=1-7, to=3-7]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-1, to=1-3, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-1, to=1-3, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-3, to=1-5, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-3, to=1-5, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-5, to=1-7, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-5, to=1-7, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
\end{tikzcd}\] for any choice of canonical context homotopy equivalences: \[\begin{aligned}\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle&:\delta'''\to\delta^{iv}\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}''\;\rangle&:\delta'\to\delta'''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'''\;\rangle&:\delta''\to\delta^{iv}\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}^{iv}\;\rangle&:\gamma' \to\gamma''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{v}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}^{v}\;\rangle&:\omega' \to\omega''
\end{aligned}\] where the propositional commutativity of the inner square is a consequence of \autoref{transitivity} and \autoref{only one canonical context homotopy equivalence}. Hence: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'':\Gamma''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(f'(\gamma''))):\Omega'']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\gamma'))):\Omega']$$ and we are done. This operation is associative and the identity of $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ is the class $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma]$, hence $\mathcal{C}$ is actually a category.

We observe that a terminal object of $\mathcal{C}$ is the class $[\;\_:\_\;]$ represented by the empty context. In fact, whenever we are given an object $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ of $\mathcal{C}$, an arrow $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\;\_:\_\;]$ is the one represented by the judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_:\_$. Moreover, it is unique: if we are given an arrow $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\;\_:\_\;]$ represented by some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_:\_$, then the diagram:  \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma \\
	& {\_} \\
	{\gamma'}
	\arrow["\textnormal{\texttt{c}}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\_}"', from=3-1, to=2-2, bend right]
	\arrow["{\_}", from=1-1, to=2-2, bend left]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes (for every $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ canonical $\gamma\to\gamma'$) even strictly by the terminality of $\_$ in the category of contexts of $\textnormal{PTT}$, hence $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_$ represents $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_]$. \endproof
