context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
\label{semantic context cat}
    There is a category $\mathcal{C}$ such that: \begin{itemize}
        \item the objects of $\mathcal{C}$ are the equivalence classes $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ of h-elementary contexts;
        \item the arrows $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\delta:\Delta]$ of $\mathcal{C}$ are the equivalence classes $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']$ of morphisms of contexts $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta'$ where $[\gamma':\Gamma']=[\gamma]$ and $[\delta':\Delta']=[\delta]$;
        \item if we are given arrows:\begin{center} $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']:[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\delta:\Delta]$ and $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']:[\delta:\Delta]\to[\omega:\Omega]$ \end{center} then, whenever $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is a canonical homotopy equivalence $\delta'\to\delta''$, the composition arrow: \begin{center} $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']\circ[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']:[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\omega:\Omega]$ \end{center} is the arrow $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\gamma'))):\Omega'].$
    \end{itemize} Moreover, the category $\mathcal{C}$ has a terminal object.
\proof Observe that the composition operation is in fact well-defined: if we are given: \begin{center}$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'':\Gamma''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f'(\gamma''):\Delta''']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']$ and $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta^{iv}:\Delta^{iv}\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\delta^{iv}):\Omega'']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']$\end{center} then: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{\gamma'} && {\delta'} && {\delta''} && {\omega'} \\
	\\
	{\gamma''} && {\delta'''} && {\delta^{iv}} && {\omega''}
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''(\delta')}"{description}, from=1-3, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}(\gamma')}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{f'(\gamma'')}"', from=3-1, to=3-3]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\delta')}", from=1-3, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\delta''')}"', from=3-3, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{f(\gamma')}", from=1-1, to=1-3]
	\arrow["{g(\delta'')}", from=1-5, to=1-7]
	\arrow["{g'(\delta^{iv})}"', from=3-5, to=3-7]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''(\delta'')}"{description}, from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{v}(\omega')}", from=1-7, to=3-7]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-1, to=1-3, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-1, to=1-3, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-3, to=1-5, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-3, to=1-5, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift left=0.5, no head, from=3-5, to=1-7, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
	\arrow[shift right=0.5, no head, from=3-5, to=1-7, shorten <=27pt, shorten >=27pt]
\end{tikzcd}\] for any choice of canonical context homotopy equivalences: \[\begin{aligned}\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle&:\delta'''\to\delta^{iv}\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}''\;\rangle&:\delta'\to\delta'''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'''\;\rangle&:\delta''\to\delta^{iv}\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}^{iv}\;\rangle&:\gamma' \to\gamma''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{v}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}^{v}\;\rangle&:\omega' \to\omega''
\end{aligned}\] where the propositional commutativity of the inner square is a consequence of \autoref{transitivity} and \autoref{only one canonical context homotopy equivalence}. Hence: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'':\Gamma''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g'(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(f'(\gamma''))):\Omega'']=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\gamma'))):\Omega']$$ and we are done. This operation is associative and the identity of $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ is the class $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma]$, hence $\mathcal{C}$ is actually a category.

We observe that a terminal object of $\mathcal{C}$ is the class $[\;\_:\_\;]$ represented by the empty context. In fact, whenever we are given an object $[\gamma:\Gamma]$ of $\mathcal{C}$, an arrow $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\;\_:\_\;]$ is the one represented by the judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_:\_$. Moreover, it is unique: if we are given an arrow $[\gamma:\Gamma]\to[\;\_:\_\;]$ represented by some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma':\Gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_:\_$, then the diagram:  \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma \\
	& {\_} \\
	{\gamma'}
	\arrow["\textnormal{\texttt{c}}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\_}"', from=3-1, to=2-2, bend right]
	\arrow["{\_}", from=1-1, to=2-2, bend left]
\end{tikzcd}\] commutes (for every $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ canonical $\gamma\to\gamma'$) even strictly by the terminality of $\_$ in the category of contexts of $\textnormal{PTT}$, hence $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_$ represents $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\_]$. \endproof

entries.lean_statement:
theorem presheaf_of_semantic_types
    {alpha : Type*} [Category alpha] (tp : alpha ⥤ alpha) (A : alpha → Type*) (gamma delta omega : alpha)
    (c c' c'' c''' c_iv : alpha → alpha) (f : delta ⥤ gamma) (g : omega ⥤ delta)
    (h_comp : ∀ x, tp (tp x) = tp x)
    (h_id : ∀ x, tp x = x)
    (h_eq1 : A (c''' (g (c'' (c' (f (c gamma)))))) = A (c''' (g (c_iv (f (c gamma))))))
    (h_c : ∀ x, c x = x)
    (h_c' : ∀ x, c' x = x)
    (h_c'' : ∀ x, c'' x = x)
    (h_c''' : ∀ x, c''' x = x)
    (h_c_iv : ∀ x, c_iv x = x)
    (h_f : f = 𝟭 _)
    (h_g : g = 𝟭 _) : tp gamma = gamma

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{presheaf of semantic types}
    The mapping $\textsc{tp}$ is a functor. \proof\text{ }\begin{itemize}
    \item Let us consider the diagram: $$[\gamma]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\gamma'):\Delta']}[\delta]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;g(\delta''):\Omega']}[\omega]$$ and let $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\omega''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\omega''):\textsc{Type}]$ be in $\textsc{tp}[\omega]$. Let us consider canonical context homotopy equivalences:\[\begin{aligned}\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle&:\gamma\to\gamma'\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle&:\delta'\to\delta\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}''\;\rangle&:\delta\to\delta''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'''\;\rangle&:\omega' \to\omega'''\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}^{iv}\;\rangle&:\delta' \to\delta''.
\end{aligned}\] Then, to verify that $\textsc{tp}$ preserves the composition, it is enough to verify that: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''(g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}''(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(f(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)))))))]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'''(g(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}^{iv}(f(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))))))]$$ which is in fact true by \autoref{transitivity} and \autoref{lemma z}.

\item Let $[\gamma]$ be a context and let $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}]$ be in $\textsc{tp}[\gamma]$. As $[\gamma']=[\gamma]$, the morphism $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma'$ represents the identity of $[\gamma]$ (see 2. of \autoref{giocare}). Let us consider canonical context homotopy equivalences: \[\begin{aligned}\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle&:\gamma'\to\gamma'\\
\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}'\;\rangle&:\gamma'\to\gamma'.
\end{aligned}\] Then $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}'(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma')))]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma')]$ by \autoref{semi destiny}, hence $\textsc{tp}$ preserves the identities and we are done.\qedhere
\end{itemize}
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.lean_statement:
theorem presheaf_of_semantic_types
    {alpha : Type*} [Category alpha] (tp : alpha ⥤ alpha) (A : alpha → Type*) (gamma delta omega : alpha)
    (c c' c'' c''' c_iv : alpha → alpha) (f : delta ⥤ gamma) (g : omega ⥤ delta)
    (h_comp : ∀ x, tp (tp x) = tp x)
    (h_id : ∀ x, tp x = x)
    (h_eq1 : A (c''' (g (c'' (c' (f (c gamma)))))) = A (c''' (g (c_iv (f (c gamma))))))
    (h_c : ∀ x, c x = x)
    (h_c' : ∀ x, c' x = x)
    (h_c'' : ∀ x, c'' x = x)
    (h_c''' : ∀ x, c''' x = x)
    (h_c_iv : ∀ x, c_iv x = x)
    (h_f : f = 𝟭 _)
    (h_g : g = 𝟭 _) : tp gamma = gamma

context_pack.section: The display map family
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{prop}\label{display map family} The family $P$ is natural and cartesian. \proof\text{ } \begin{itemize} \item \textit{Naturality.} Let us consider an arrow: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'':\Delta''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta''):\Gamma'']:([\delta:\Delta],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta'):\textsc{Type}])\to ([\gamma:\Gamma],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}])$$ in $\int\textsc{tp}$. By 3. of \autoref{giocare}, we can assume without loss of generality that $\delta''\equiv\delta'$ and $\gamma''\equiv\gamma'$. If $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ is canonical between $\gamma'$ and itself, then: \[\begin{aligned} \relax [\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma'):\textsc{Type}]&=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma')):\textsc{Type}]\\ [\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;B(\delta'):\textsc{Type}]&=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\delta'))):\textsc{Type}] \end{aligned}\] Then the images of $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta)':\Gamma']$ via the semantic context extension and via the projection admit the presentations: \[\begin{aligned} \relax [\delta',x':A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(f(\delta')))]&\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta',x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta'),x']}[\gamma',x:A(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma'))]\\ [\delta']&\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta')]}[\gamma'] \end{aligned}\] and we are left to verify that they commute with $P$, in order to conclude the naturality of $P$ itself. \label{category with attributes}
The category $\mathcal{C}$ of \autoref{semantic context cat}, together with the presheaf $\textsc{tp}$ of \autoref{presheaf of semantic types}, the semantic context extension $-.-$ of \autoref{semantic context extension} and the natural transformation $P$ of \autoref{display map family}, forms a category with attributes $\boldsymbol{\mathfrak{C}}:=(\mathcal{C},\tp,-.-,P)$.

The remaining conditions of \autoref{semantic extensional identity types}---i.e. \textnormal{extensionality} and \textnormal{compatibility with the substitution}---are satisfied by the above choice of: $$\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}\text{ and }\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ hence $\boldsymbol{\mathfrak{C}}$ is equipped with semantic extensional identity types.
\proof\text{ }
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textit{Extensionality}. Let us consider two semantic terms: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]\textnormal{ and }[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,b(\gamma):A(\gamma)]$$ of the semantic type $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$ in the semantic context $[\gamma:\Gamma]$. They admit such a presentation because of 3. of \autoref{giocare} and \autoref{nuovo gioco}. Now, if we consider $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]$ as an arrow of $\int\textsc{tp}$ of source $([\gamma],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])$ and target $([\gamma,x],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])$, its semantic context extension: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ is: $$[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),x]}[\gamma,x,y]$$ hence the arrow: $$[\gamma]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,b(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}[\gamma,x,y]$$ of \autoref{encode two terms in one morphism} happens to be represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)$, because of 4. of \autoref{giocare}.
    
    By \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}, the semantic type: $$\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)](\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]})=\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)]([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;x=y:\textsc{Type}])$$ in semantic context $[\gamma]$ admits the presentation: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;a(\gamma)=b(\gamma):\textsc{Type}.$$ With this presentation, if we are given a semantic term of this semantic type in context $[\gamma]$, i.e. a section $[\gamma]\to[\gamma,p':a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]$ of the corresponding display map $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p':a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma]$, then by \autoref{nuovo gioco} it admits a representative of the form $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$. Hence $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma)]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,b(\gamma)]$, by 1. of \autoref{giocare}. We are left to verify that: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]=\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}^{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}.$$ As the diagram (where $p'':a(\gamma)=a(\gamma)$): \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma &&&& {\gamma,x} \\
	\\
	{\gamma,p''} &&&& {\gamma,x,y,p}
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma)}", from=1-1, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,x,x,\mathsf{r}(x)}", from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),a(\gamma),p''}", from=3-1, to=3-5]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes even judgementally and ${\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),a(\gamma),p''}$ represents the extension: $$(\;[\gamma]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}[\gamma,x,y]\;).\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ then $\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}^{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}$ is represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))$ and we are left to verify that: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))].$$
    
    Let us consider a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ and let $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ and such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):\phi(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ (see \autoref{there are context identities} and \autoref{there are identities}). Now, if: \[\begin{aligned}
    \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma&\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{r}_1(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,a(\gamma))\bullet a(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}:\phi(\gamma,a(\gamma))=a(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))\\
    \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma&\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{r}_2(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,a(\gamma))\bullet a(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\bullet p(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))
    :\phi(\gamma,a(\gamma))=b(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))
    \end{aligned}\] then the corresponding $\langle\;\phi^=\;|\;\psi^=\;\rangle$ of \autoref{id equiv} happens to be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $a(\gamma)=a(\gamma)$ and $a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ (see the notion of canonical homotopy equivalence in \autoref{section2.3.VI}, as well as \autoref{crucial example}) and by propositional groupoidality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi^=(\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma)))=p(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)).$$ Therefore, the diagram: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma &&& {\gamma,p''} \\
	\\
	\gamma &&& {\gamma,p'}
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma)}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow["\textnormal{\texttt{c}}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}},\phi^=}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes propositionally and, by the notion of canonical context homotopy equivalence, the morphism of contexts $\textnormal{\texttt{c}},\phi^=$ is the first component of a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\gamma,p''\to\gamma,p'$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}). Therefore $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma)$ represent the same morphism of semantic contexts i.e. the same arrow of $\mathcal{C}$ and we are done. 
 
    \item\textit{Compatibility with the substitution}. If we are given a morphism of contexts: $$[\delta:\Delta]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma]}[\gamma:\Gamma]$$ (such a representative exists by 3. of \autoref{giocare}) then the semantic context extension $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x':A(f(\delta))]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x']}[\gamma,x:A(\gamma)]$$ and the further semantic context extension: $$([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x',y':A(f(\delta))]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x',y']}[\gamma,x,y:A(\gamma)].$$ Hence, by \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}, the type: $$\textsc{tp}(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ in context $[\delta,x',y']$ is represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;x'=y'$, which is a representative of $\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta)):\textsc{Type}]}$, and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta)):\textsc{Type}$ represents the type: $$(\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma])[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ in context $\delta$, again by \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}. We conclude that: \begin{center}$\textsc{tp}(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}=$\\$=\mathsf{id}_{(\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma])[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}.$\end{center} Now, the semantic context extension: $$(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x',y',p':x'=y']\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y',p'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x',y',p']}[\gamma,x,y,p:x=y]$$ hence we are left to verify that: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{[\delta,x']} &&& {[\gamma,x]} \\
	\\
	{[\delta,x',y',p']} &&& {[\gamma,x,y,p]}
	\arrow["{\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta))]}}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma)]}}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{[f(\delta),x']}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{[f(\delta),x',y',p']}", from=3-1, to=3-4] \end{tikzcd}\] commutes in $\mathcal{C}$. By 1. and 4. of \autoref{giocare}, we are done since the diagram of the representatives: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{\delta,x'} &&& {\gamma,x} \\
	\\
	{\delta,x',y',p'} &&& {\gamma,x,y,p}
	\arrow["{\delta,x',x',\mathsf{r}(x')}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\gamma,x,x,\mathsf{r}(x)}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{f(\delta),x'}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{f(\delta),x',y',p'}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow[shift right=0.7, shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow[shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow[shift left=0.7, shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes even judgmentally.\qedhere
\end{itemize}
\endproof

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
The remaining conditions of \autoref{semantic extensional identity types}---i.e. \textnormal{extensionality} and \textnormal{compatibility with the substitution}---are satisfied by the above choice of: $$\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}\text{ and }\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ hence $\boldsymbol{\mathfrak{C}}$ is equipped with semantic extensional identity types.
\proof\text{ }
\begin{itemize}
    \item \textit{Extensionality}. Let us consider two semantic terms: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]\textnormal{ and }[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,b(\gamma):A(\gamma)]$$ of the semantic type $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$ in the semantic context $[\gamma:\Gamma]$. They admit such a presentation because of 3. of \autoref{giocare} and \autoref{nuovo gioco}. Now, if we consider $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]$ as an arrow of $\int\textsc{tp}$ of source $([\gamma],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])$ and target $([\gamma,x],[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])$, its semantic context extension: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ is: $$[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),x]}[\gamma,x,y]$$ hence the arrow: $$[\gamma]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,b(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}[\gamma,x,y]$$ of \autoref{encode two terms in one morphism} happens to be represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)$, because of 4. of \autoref{giocare}.
    
    By \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}, the semantic type: $$\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)](\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]})=\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),b(\gamma)]([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x,y\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;x=y:\textsc{Type}])$$ in semantic context $[\gamma]$ admits the presentation: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;a(\gamma)=b(\gamma):\textsc{Type}.$$ With this presentation, if we are given a semantic term of this semantic type in context $[\gamma]$, i.e. a section $[\gamma]\to[\gamma,p':a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]$ of the corresponding display map $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p':a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma]$, then by \autoref{nuovo gioco} it admits a representative of the form $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$ for some judgement $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$. Hence $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma)]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,b(\gamma)]$, by 1. of \autoref{giocare}. We are left to verify that: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]=\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}^{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}.$$ As the diagram (where $p'':a(\gamma)=a(\gamma)$): \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma &&&& {\gamma,x} \\
	\\
	{\gamma,p''} &&&& {\gamma,x,y,p}
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma)}", from=1-1, to=1-5]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))}"', from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,x,x,\mathsf{r}(x)}", from=1-5, to=3-5]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),a(\gamma),p''}", from=3-1, to=3-5]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes even judgementally and ${\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,p''\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma),a(\gamma),p''}$ represents the extension: $$(\;[\gamma]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}[\gamma,x]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}[\gamma,x,y]\;).\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ then $\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}^{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma:\Gamma,a(\gamma):A(\gamma)]}$ is represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))$ and we are left to verify that: $$[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma):a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)]=[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))].$$
    
    Let us consider a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ between $\gamma$ and itself such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha(\gamma):\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)=\gamma$ and let $\langle\;\phi\;|\;\psi\;\rangle$ be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $A(\gamma)$ and itself relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ and such that $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\alpha_1(\gamma,x):\phi(\gamma,x)=\alpha(\gamma)^*x$ (see \autoref{there are context identities} and \autoref{there are identities}). Now, if: \[\begin{aligned}
    \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma&\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{r}_1(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,a(\gamma))\bullet a(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}:\phi(\gamma,a(\gamma))=a(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))\\
    \boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma&\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\mathsf{r}_2(\gamma)\equiv \alpha_1(\gamma,a(\gamma))\bullet a(\alpha(\gamma))^{-1}\bullet p(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))
    :\phi(\gamma,a(\gamma))=b(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma))
    \end{aligned}\] then the corresponding $\langle\;\phi^=\;|\;\psi^=\;\rangle$ of \autoref{id equiv} happens to be a canonical homotopy equivalence between $a(\gamma)=a(\gamma)$ and $a(\gamma)=b(\gamma)$ relative to $\langle\;\textnormal{\texttt{c}}\;|\;\textnormal{\texttt{d}}\;\rangle$ (see the notion of canonical homotopy equivalence in \autoref{section2.3.VI}, as well as \autoref{crucial example}) and by propositional groupoidality: $$\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\phi^=(\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma)))=p(\textnormal{\texttt{c}}(\gamma)).$$ Therefore, the diagram: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	\gamma &&& {\gamma,p''} \\
	\\
	\gamma &&& {\gamma,p'}
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma)}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow["\textnormal{\texttt{c}}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\textnormal{\texttt{c}},\phi^=}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes propositionally and, by the notion of canonical context homotopy equivalence, the morphism of contexts $\textnormal{\texttt{c}},\phi^=$ is the first component of a canonical context homotopy equivalence $\gamma,p''\to\gamma,p'$ (see \autoref{context homotopy equivalence expansion}). Therefore $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,\mathsf{r}(a(\gamma))$ and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\gamma,p(\gamma)$ represent the same morphism of semantic contexts i.e. the same arrow of $\mathcal{C}$ and we are done. 
 
    \item\textit{Compatibility with the substitution}. If we are given a morphism of contexts: $$[\delta:\Delta]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma]}[\gamma:\Gamma]$$ (such a representative exists by 3. of \autoref{giocare}) then the semantic context extension $[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x':A(f(\delta))]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x']}[\gamma,x:A(\gamma)]$$ and the further semantic context extension: $$([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x',y':A(f(\delta))]\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x',y']}[\gamma,x,y:A(\gamma)].$$ Hence, by \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}, the type: $$\textsc{tp}(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ in context $[\delta,x',y']$ is represented by $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;x'=y'$, which is a representative of $\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta)):\textsc{Type}]}$, and $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta)):\textsc{Type}$ represents the type: $$(\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma])[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]$$ in context $\delta$, again by \autoref{there are context identities}, by \autoref{semi destiny} and by \autoref{small reindexing}. We conclude that: \begin{center}$\textsc{tp}(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}])\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}=$\\$=\mathsf{id}_{(\textsc{tp}[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma])[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}.$\end{center} Now, the semantic context extension: $$(([\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta):\Gamma].[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma,x:A(\gamma)\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]).\mathsf{id}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma):\textsc{Type}]}$$ admits the presentation: $$[\delta,x',y',p':x'=y']\xrightarrow{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta,x',y',p'\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;f(\delta),x',y',p']}[\gamma,x,y,p:x=y]$$ hence we are left to verify that: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{[\delta,x']} &&& {[\gamma,x]} \\
	\\
	{[\delta,x',y',p']} &&& {[\gamma,x,y,p]}
	\arrow["{\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\delta\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(f(\delta))]}}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\mathsf{r}_{[\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;A(\gamma)]}}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{[f(\delta),x']}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{[f(\delta),x',y',p']}", from=3-1, to=3-4] \end{tikzcd}\] commutes in $\mathcal{C}$. By 1. and 4. of \autoref{giocare}, we are done since the diagram of the representatives: \[\begin{tikzcd}
	{\delta,x'} &&& {\gamma,x} \\
	\\
	{\delta,x',y',p'} &&& {\gamma,x,y,p}
	\arrow["{\delta,x',x',\mathsf{r}(x')}"{description}, from=1-1, to=3-1]
	\arrow["{\gamma,x,x,\mathsf{r}(x)}"{description}, from=1-4, to=3-4]
	\arrow["{f(\delta),x'}", from=1-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow["{f(\delta),x',y',p'}", from=3-1, to=3-4]
	\arrow[shift right=0.7, shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow[shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\arrow[shift left=0.7, shorten <=40pt, shorten >=45pt, no head, from=3-1, to=1-4]
	\end{tikzcd}\] commutes even judgmentally.\qedhere
\end{itemize}
\endproof

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
[Conservativity]\label{main result}
Let $\gamma:\Gamma$ be an h-elementary context of $\textnormal{PTT}$ and let $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;T(\gamma:\Gamma):\textsc{Type}$ be an h-elementary type judgement of $\textnormal{PTT}$. Whenever $\textnormal{ETT}$ infers $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}|\gamma:\Gamma|\boldsymbol{\rfloor}_{\textnormal{\texttt{ext}}}\;t(|\gamma:\Gamma|):|T(\gamma)|$, then $\textnormal{PTT}$ infers $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}\gamma:\Gamma\boldsymbol{\rfloor}\;\tilde{t}(\gamma:\Gamma):T(\gamma:\Gamma)$ and $\textnormal{ETT}$ infers $\boldsymbol{\lfloor}|\gamma:\Gamma|\boldsymbol{\rfloor}_{\textnormal{\texttt{ext}}}\;|\tilde{t}(\gamma:\Gamma)|\equiv t(|\gamma:\Gamma|)$.
semantic_equivalence_artifact.normalized_natural_language_theorem: Assumptions: γ:Γ is an h-elementary context of PTT; ⌊γ:Γ⌋ T(γ:Γ):Type is an h-elementary type judgement of PTT; ETT infers ⌊|γ:Γ|⌋_ext t(|γ:Γ|):|T(γ)| Conclusion: PTT infers ⌊γ:Γ⌋ t̃(γ:Γ):T(γ:Γ) and ETT infers ⌊|γ:Γ|⌋_ext |t̃(γ:Γ)| ≡ t(|γ:Γ|)
